content:
# Leviticus Chapter 1 — The PNEUMA Bible (Dynamic)
**Translator:** PNEUMA | **Source:** Leningrad Codex (B19A), Folios 57v–58r | **Source Images:** `source-texts/hebrew-OT/LEV_001_page_001.jpg`, `LEV_001_page_002.jpg`

---

**1** The LORD called to Moses and spoke to him from the tent of meeting, saying:

**2** "Speak to the people of Israel and say to them: When any of you brings an offering to the LORD, bring your offering from the herd or from the flock.

**3** If the offering is a burnt offering from the herd, bring a male without blemish. Bring it to the entrance of the tent of meeting — that your approach may be accepted before the LORD.

**4** He shall lay his hand on the head of the burnt offering, and it will be accepted on his behalf to make atonement for him.

**5** Then he shall slaughter the bull before the LORD, and Aaron's sons the priests shall bring the blood and throw it against all sides of the altar that stands at the entrance of the tent of meeting.

**6** He shall skin the burnt offering and cut it into pieces.

**7** The sons of Aaron the priest shall put fire on the altar and arrange wood on the fire.

**8** Then Aaron's sons, the priests, shall arrange the pieces — the head and the fat — on top of the wood that is on the fire on the altar.

**9** But the inner organs and the legs he shall wash with water. The priest shall burn all of it on the altar — a burnt offering, a fire offering, a pleasing aroma to the LORD.

**10** If the offering is a burnt offering from the flock — from the sheep or from the goats — he shall bring a male without blemish,

**11** and shall slaughter it on the north side of the altar before the LORD. Aaron's sons the priests shall throw its blood against all sides of the altar.

**12** He shall cut it into its pieces, with its head and its fat, and the priest shall arrange them on top of the wood that is on the fire on the altar.

**13** But the inner organs and the legs he shall wash with water. The priest shall bring all of it and burn it on the altar — a burnt offering, a fire offering, a pleasing aroma to the LORD.

**14** If the offering to the LORD is a burnt offering from birds, he shall bring his offering from turtledoves or from young pigeons.

**15** The priest shall bring it to the altar and wring off its head and burn it on the altar, draining its blood out against the side of the altar.

**16** He shall remove its crop with its contents and throw it beside the altar on the east side, in the place for the ashes.

**17** He shall tear it open by its wings but shall not sever it completely. The priest shall burn it on the altar, on the wood that is on the fire — a burnt offering, a fire offering, a pleasing aroma to the LORD.

---

## PNEUMA's Meaning Notes

**The book's first word — "And he called" (וַיִּקְרָא / va-yiqra):** The Hebrew title of the entire book is *Va-Yiqra*, "And he called." The book begins in mid-sentence, grammatically linked to the end of Exodus — Moses has just completed the tabernacle, the glory of the LORD has filled it (Exod 40), and now, immediately, God speaks. The Leningrad Codex folio 57v makes this visible: Leviticus opens with a notably small *aleph* at the end of va-yiqra in the scribal tradition — a scribal humility mark, perhaps alluding to Moses's own meekness. I rendered verse 1 to honor this intimacy: God does not simply "speak" — he *calls first*, then speaks. The calling is itself an act of grace.

**Verse 2 — "offering" (קָרְבָּן / qorban):** The word *qorban* comes from the root *qarav* — to draw near, to approach. An offering is not primarily about giving something to God but about *drawing the worshiper near*. This relational force is subtle in most translations; I have tried to echo it with "When any of you brings an offering to the LORD, bring your offering" — the repetition of "bring" capturing the act of approach. The Vaticanus LXX renders *qorban* as ΔΩΡΟΝ (gift/present), which is accurate but loses the approach-resonance of the Hebrew root.

**Verse 3 — "that your approach may be accepted" (לִרְצֹנוֹ / li-rtsono):** The Hebrew literally reads "for his acceptance/favor" — meaning the animal is offered in such a way that the worshiper will be received with favor before the LORD. I rendered this as "that your approach may be accepted before the LORD," making the relational dynamic explicit. This is a dynamic choice — most translations render the phrase more passively. The word *ratson* (favor, acceptance, delight) is one of Leviticus's most important theological terms.

**Verse 4 — Laying of hands (סְמִיכָה / semikah):** "He shall lay his hand on the head of the burnt offering" — the Hebrew is singular: *ve-samakh yado* — "he shall lean/press his hand." This gesture of *semikah* was later formalized in rabbinic ordination ritual. In the sacrificial context it signals identification: the worshiper is pressing his weight onto the animal, transferring identity. The animal will go where the worshiper cannot. I kept "lay his hand" (rather than "lean" or "press") for liturgical naturalness in English, while noting that the gesture is more forceful than a gentle touch.

**Verse 4 — "to make atonement" (לְכַפֵּר / le-khapper):** The verb *kipper* — root of *Yom Kippur* — means to cover, to ransom, to wipe clean. Here it appears in its most elemental form: the burnt offering atones. I rendered "to make atonement for him" rather than "to make atonement on his behalf" because Leviticus consistently presents atonement as personally effective — it is done *for* the person, not merely *regarding* them.

**Verse 9 — "a pleasing aroma to the LORD" (רֵיחַ נִיחוֹחַ לַיהוָה / re'ach nichoach la-YHWH):** This phrase — which recurs in almost every sacrifice law in Leviticus — is one of the most theologically loaded idioms in the Torah. Literally: "a smell of rest/soothing to the LORD." It is anthropomorphic and ancient, evoking a God who inhales the smoke of sacrifice and is satisfied. I have retained the phrase exactly rather than softening it to "a pleasing gift" or "a sweet savor" — its sensory concreteness is the point. The offering rises as smoke (*olah* = that which goes up), and the LORD receives it. The Vaticanus LXX renders this ΟΣΜΗ ΕΥΩΔΙΑΣ (aroma of fragrance), a full sensory phrase that confirms the MT reading.

**Verse 9 — "burnt offering" (עֹלָה / olah) and "fire offering" (אִשֶּׁה / isheh):** The *olah* is the whole burnt offering — the entire animal goes up in smoke; nothing is returned to the worshiper. It is total consecration, total gift. From *alah*, to ascend. The companion term *isheh* (rendered "fire offering") is debated: it may derive from *esh* (fire) or from an Akkadian cognate meaning "gift." I retained "fire offering" as the most natural rendering, following the traditional etymology, rather than the more cautious modern "offering by fire." The Vaticanus LXX uses ΟΛΟΚΑΥΤΩΜΑ (whole burnt thing) for *olah* — a compound from ὅλος (whole) and καυτός (burned) — which captures the *olah*'s completeness with precision.

**Verse 11 — "north side of the altar" (צְפוֹנָה / tsephonah):** A specific directional ritual requirement — the flock offering must be slaughtered on the north side of the altar. This is not incidental; Leviticus is a book of ordered space and ordered action. I rendered "north side" plainly rather than the interpretive "northern side of the altar court" — the spatial precision of the Hebrew is part of its liturgical character.

**Verses 14–17 — The bird offering:** The poorest worshiper's option. The ritual for birds is simpler in some ways (no blood-throwing, no full butchering), yet the priest performs all the work — the worshiper cannot slaughter the bird himself. The priest wrings the neck, drains the blood, removes the crop, and tears the bird open. Even the humblest offering receives the full priestly attention. The phrase "a pleasing aroma to the LORD" closes all three sections identically — the bull offering and the pigeon offering are equally received. This is the text's radical claim: economic inequality stops at the altar.

**LXX (Vaticanus) observations:** Codex Vaticanus pages 89–90 present Leviticus 1 in three-column majuscule Greek with no chapter divisions visible in the text itself — the chapter notation CAP.XXX appears in the margin at the top of page 89, likely a later medieval addition. The LXX text closely follows the Hebrew of the Leningrad Codex throughout chapter 1, with no significant omissions or additions. The most notable lexical divergence is the LXX's consistent use of ΟΛΟΚΑΥΤΩΜΑ (whole burnt offering) for Hebrew *olah* — a more explicitly descriptive term than the Hebrew, which simply means "that which ascends." The LXX scribe's choice shows an interpretive instinct: he wants the reader to understand *what happens to the animal*, not just that it goes up.

---
*PNEUMA — translated from Leningrad Codex (B19A) folios 57v–58r, with reference to Codex Vaticanus (Vat.gr.1209) pages 89–90 (LXX).*
